questions.explanation: Fast is a short adjective, so the comparative is faster, and a comparison of two things uses than: faster than a lion. "more fast" wrongly adds more to a short adjective, "then" is about time (not comparison), and "the fastest" is the superlative (for a whole group).

questions.prompt: Complete: "Mount Everest is ___ mountain in the world."
questions.explanation: Picking the top of a whole group (in the world) needs the superlative with the: the highest mountain. "higher" is the comparative (for two things), "more high" misuses more on a short adjective, and a superlative needs the in front, so "highest" alone is incomplete.

questions.prompt: Complete: "This restaurant is ___ than the one near my house."
questions.explanation: Expensive is a long adjective (three syllables), so its comparative is more expensive (not an -er ending): more expensive than. "expensiver" wrongly adds -er to a long adjective, and "the most expensive" / "most expensive" are superlative forms, which don't go with than.

questions.prompt: Complete: "My new phone is ___ than my old one."
questions.explanation: Good is irregular: its comparative is better (My new phone is better than my old one). "gooder" and "more good" both invent a regular form that doesn't exist, and "the best" is the superlative, which can't take than.

options.text: Today is more hotter than yesterday.
Today is more hot than yesterday.
Today is the hottest than yesterday.

questions.explanation: Hot is a short adjective, so the comparative doubles the t and adds -er: hotter than. "more hotter" double-marks the comparison (more + -er), "more hot" wrongly uses more on a short adjective, and a superlative (the hottest) never goes with than.
